vulnerability.title: Disable Ctrl-Alt-Del Reboot Activation
vulnerability.description: By default, SystemD will reboot the system if the Ctrl-Alt-Del key sequence is pressed. To configure the system to ignore the Ctrl-Alt-Del key sequence from the command line instead of rebooting the system, do either of the following: ln -sf /dev/null /etc/systemd/system/ctrl-alt-del.target or systemctl mask ctrl-alt-del.target Do not simply delete the /usr/lib/systemd/system/ctrl-alt-del.service file, as this file may be restored during future system updates.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then if { rpm --quiet -q kernel rpm-ostree bootc && ! rpm --quiet -q openshift-kubelet && { [ -f "/run/.containerenv" ] || [ -f "/.containerenv" ]; }; } ; then systemctl disable ctrl-alt-del.target systemctl mask ctrl-alt-del.target else systemctl disable --now ctrl-alt-del.target systemctl mask --now ctrl-alt-del.target fi else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage_decision.reason: Disabling Ctrl-Alt-Del via systemd mask only prevents accidental reboot via keyboard shortcut; it does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services and poses no risk of lockout.

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
We need to produce the necessary tool calls to disable Ctrl-Alt-Del reboot activation by masking the target.

We need to:

1. ln -sf /dev/null /etc/systemd/system/ctrl-alt-del.target
OR systemctl mask ctrl-alt-del.target

We must decide which approach. Both achieve same effect: linking the target unit to /dev/null disables it; systemctl mask also masks it (makes it not start). The remediation suggests either.

We need to output tool calls in the specified format: each function call as a separate line? The spec: we have functions: review_plan, run_cmd, read_file, write_file. We need to produce the fix: likely we just need to run a command (ln or systemctl). We don't need to review or read/write files. So we will call run_cmd with appropriate command.

We must output something like:

We need to produce JSON with plan_description and commands array containing exactly one command string to disable Ctrl-Alt-Del reboot activation.

The fix: mask the systemd target ctrl-alt-del.target to prevent reboot on Ctrl-Alt-Del.

Systemd has a target unit that when triggered leads to reboot? Actually systemd has a special unit ctrl-alt-del.target that is triggered by the keyboard shortcut Ctrl-Alt-Del and causes reboot. To disable, we mask that target so it cannot be started.

Thus we need to run: systemctl mask ctrl-alt-del.target

We need to provide plan_description describing that we mask the systemd target ctrl-alt-del.target to disable the reboot activation.

Thus JSON:

{
  "plan_description": "Mask the systemd ctrl-alt-del.target unit to prevent the Ctrl-Alt-Del keyboard shortcut from triggering a system reboot.",
  "commands": [
    "systemctl mask ctrl-alt-del.target"
  ]
}

Make sure valid JSON.

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
We are given the task to remediate the OpenSCAP finding: "Disable Ctrl-Alt-Del Reboot Activation" on Rocky Linux 10.

The remediation steps are provided in the context:

  "Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then if { rpm --quiet -q kernel rpm-ostree bootc && ! rpm --quiet -q openshift-kubelet && { [ -f "/run/.containerenv" ] || [ -f "/.containerenv" ]; }; } ; then systemctl disable ctrl-alt-del.target systemctl mask ctrl-alt-del.target else systemctl disable --now ctrl-alt-del.target systemctl mask --now ctrl-alt-del.target fi else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi"

We must use the provided tools: run_cmd, read_file, write_file.

Steps:

1. We must check if we are on a platform where the remediation is applicable. The condition is:
   if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then if { rpm --quiet -q kernel rpm-ostree bootc && ! rpm --quiet -q openshift-kubelet && { [ -f "/run/.containerenv" ] || [ -f "/.containerenv" ]; }; }

   This condition is complex. We break it down:

   Part A: rpm --quiet -q kernel-core
   Part B: if { rpm --quiet -q kernel rpm-ostree bootc && ! rpm --quiet -q openshift-kubelet && { [ -f "/run/.containerenv" ] || [ -f "/.containerenv" ]; }; }

   So the entire condition is: Part A AND Part B.

   We can write:

      if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then
          if { condition for Part B }; then
              ... remediation steps ...
          else
              ... not applicable ...
      else
          ... not applicable ...

   However, note that the condition in the remediation script is written as a single line. We must interpret it as:

      if (rpm --quiet -q kernel-core) and ( ... condition for Part B ... ); then
          ... remediation steps ...
      else
          ... not applicable ...

   But note: the condition for Part B is: 
        { rpm --quiet -q kernel rpm-ostree bootc && ! rpm --quiet -q openshift-kubelet && { [ -f "/run/.containerenv" ] || [ -f "/.containerenv" ]; }; }

   This is a set of conditions that must all be true for the inner braces.

   So Part B is true if:
        (rpm --quiet -q kernel) AND (rpm --quiet -q kernel-ostree bootc) AND (NOT (rpm --quiet -q openshift-kubelet)) AND ( [ -f "/run/.containerenv" ] OR [ -f "/.containerenv" ] )

   Therefore, the entire condition is:
        (rpm --quiet -q kernel-core) AND
        (rpm --quiet -q kernel) AND
        (rpm --quiet -q kernel-ostree bootc) AND
        (NOT (rpm --quiet -q openshift-kubelet)) AND
        ( [ -f "/run/.containerenv" ] OR [ -f "/.containerenv" ] )

   We can check each part with read_file and then use conditional logic.

   However, note: the condition might be simplified by the fact that we are on Rocky Linux 10. We can assume that the kernel package is installed? But we must check.

   We are going to check:

      Step 1.1: Check if kernel-core package is installed (quietly) -> rpm -q kernel-core -> if not, then not applicable.

      Step 1.2: If Step 1.1 passed, then check the inner condition (Part B):

          Step 1.2.1: Check if kernel package is installed (quietly) -> rpm -q kernel
          Step 1.2.2: Check if kernel-ostree package is installed (quietly) -> rpm -q kernel-ostree
          Step 1.2.3: Check if openshift-kubelet package is NOT installed (quietly) -> rpm -q openshift-kubelet (if it returns 0, then it is not installed -> condition true; if returns non-zero, then condition false)
          Step 1.2.4: Check if either /run/.containerenv OR /etc/containerenv exists (as a directory or file? The condition uses [ -f ... ] which checks for existence of a file. We can use: if [ -f "/run/.containerenv" ] || [ -f "/etc/containerenv" ]; then true.

          So Part B is true only if:
              Step 1.2.1: true (kernel installed)
              Step 1.2.2: true (kernel-ostree installed)
              Step 1.2.3: true (openshift-kubelet NOT installed)
              Step 1.2.4: true (either containerenv path exists)

      Step 2: If the entire condition (Part A and Part B) is true, then we run the remediation steps.

          The remediation steps are:

            systemctl disable ctrl-alt-del.target
            systemctl mask ctrl-alt-del.target
            else   # meaning: if the previous two commands fail, then we do:
                systemctl disable --now ctrl-alt-del.target
                systemctl mask --now ctrl-alt-del.target

          Note: the remediation script uses the keyword 'else' which in shell scripting means: if the previous command (or block) fails, then run the following.

          We can interpret:

            Run: systemctl disable ctrl-alt-del.target
            Then run: systemctl mask ctrl-alt-del.target
            If the mask command fails, then run:
                systemctl disable --now ctrl-alt-del.target
                systemctl mask --now ctrl-alt-del.target

          However, note: the remediation script does not specify what to do if the first two commands succeed. We only run the else block if the mask command fails.

      Step 3: If the condition fails at any point, we output: 
          >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done'

   Implementation:

      We'll use:

        # Check kernel-core
        if ! rpm -q kernel-core; then
            echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' >&2
            exit 1
        fi

        # Check kernel
        if ! rpm -q kernel; then
            echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' >&2
            exit 1
        fi

        # Check kernel-ostree
        if ! rpm -q kernel-ostree; then
            echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' >&2

        fi

        # Check openshift-kubelet: we want it NOT to be installed
        if rpm -q openshift-kubelet; then
            echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' >&2
            exit 1
        fi

        # Check containerenv paths: we want at least one of /run/.containerenv or /etc/containerenv to exist
        if [ ! -f "/run/.containerenv" ] && [ ! -f "/etc/containerenv" ]; then
            echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' >&2
            exit 1
        fi

        # If we passed all checks, then run the remediation steps.

        # Run: systemctl disable ctrl-alt-del.target
        run_cmd "systemctl disable ctrl-alt-del.target"
        # Check the result of the disable command? We don't check in the remediation script, we just run the mask command next.
        # Mask: systemctl mask ctrl-alt-del.target
        run_cmd "systemctl mask ctrl-alt-del.target"
        # If the mask command fails, we run the else block.
        if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
            run_cmd "systemctl disable --now ctrl-alt-del.target"
            run_cmd "systemctl mask --now ctrl-alt-del.target"
        fi

   However, note: the mask command might fail for reasons other than the target not existing? But we follow the script.

   But note: the remediation script does not specify what to do if the mask command succeeds. We only run the else block if it fails.

   Important: We must use the exact commands as specified in the remediation script.

   Let's write the code accordingly.

   Note: We are to use run_cmd for executing shell commands, read_file for reading files, and write_file for writing files.

   We don't need read_file or write_file in this remediation? We are only executing commands.

   We'll use run_cmd for the systemctl commands.

   We don't need to read or write any files for this particular remediation.

   Therefore, we can write:

        if ! rpm -q kernel-core; then
            echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' >&2
            exit 1
        fi

        if ! rpm -q kernel; then
            echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' >&2
            exit 1
        fi

        if ! rpm -q kernel-ostree; then
            echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' >&2
            exit 1
        fi

        if rpm -q openshift-kubelet; then
            echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' >&2
            exit 1
        fi

        if [ ! -f "/run/.containerenv" ] && [ ! -f "/etc/containerenv" ]; then
            echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' >&2
            exit 1
        fi

        # All checks passed
        run_cmd "systemctl disable ctrl-alt-del.target"
        run_cmd "systemctl mask ctrl-alt-del.target"
        if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
            run_cmd "systemctl disable --now ctrl-alt-del.target"
            run_cmd "systemctl mask --now ctrl-alt-del.target"
        fi

   However, note: the condition for openshift-kubelet is: we want it NOT to be installed. So if rpm -q openshift-kubelet returns 0 (meaning not installed) then we proceed. If it returns non-zero (installed) then we output not applicable.

   We did: if rpm -q openshift-kubelet; then ... -> meaning if it returns non-zero (installed) then we output not applicable.

   This is correct.

   Let's code accordingly.

   We assume that the environment is Rocky Linux 10, and we have the tools available.

   We'll write the code in the form of a shell script.

   Note: We must not output anything else.

   We'll now write the code.

   Important: We must use the exact tool names: run_cmd, read_file, write_file.

   We are not using read_file or write_file in this script, but we must declare that we have them available? The problem says we have access to them.

   We are only using run_cmd.

   We'll write:
reasoning_messages.content:
if ! rpm -q kernel-core; then
    echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' >&2
    exit 1
fi

if ! rpm -q kernel; then
    echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' >&2
    exit 1
fi

if ! rpm -q kernel-ostree; then
    echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' >&2
    exit 1
fi

if rpm -q openshift-kubelet; then
    echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' >&2
    exit 1
fi

if [ ! -f "/run/.containerenv" ] && [ ! -f "/etc/containerenv" ]; then
    echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' >&2
    exit 1
fi

run_cmd "systemctl disable ctrl-alt-del.target"
run_cmd "systemctl mask ctrl-alt-del.target"
if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
    run_cmd "systemctl disable --now ctrl-alt-del.target"
    run_cmd "systemctl mask --now ctrl-alt-del.target"
fi
